items.title: Ad spend and revenue move together
items.scenario: A marketing team tracks weekly ad spend (thousands of dollars) and revenue. When spend rises, revenue tends to rise too. Covariance s_XY is positive and large in dollar units; Pearson r ≈ 0.85 shows a strong linear link regardless of whether spend is measured in thousands or dollars.
items.explanation: Covariance reflects joint variation in original units; correlation standardizes so stakeholders can compare strength across metrics with different scales.

items.title: Temperature and ice-cream sales
items.scenario: Daily temperature and ice-cream units sold are positively associated in summer months. A heat wave week pushes both above their seasonal averages, contributing positive products (xᵢ - x̄)(yᵢ - ȳ) in the sample covariance sum.
items.explanation: Each paired deviation from the mean adds to covariance; many same-sign deviations yield positive Cov.

items.title: Feature correlation heatmap before PCA
items.scenario: An analyst builds a correlation matrix for 20 sensor features. Pairs with |r| > 0.95 flag redundancy; the covariance matrix of centered training data feeds PCA, where eigenvalues rank variance along principal directions.
items.explanation: Correlation surfaces redundant features; covariance (or centered-data SVD) drives variance decomposition downstream.

items.title: Misleading zero correlation
items.scenario: Y = X² for X symmetric around 0 (e.g., uniform on [-1, 1]). Scatter points form a U-shape, but linear correlation r ≈ 0 because positive and negative X deviations cancel in the covariance sum.
items.explanation: Pearson correlation targets linear structure; strong deterministic non-linear ties can hide in r near zero.
